statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Consider a chessboard (a grid) of size $ n \times n $ . You want to place as many chess pieces on this board as possible. These pieces behave in the following way: if we denote the top-left cell by $ (1,1) $ , then a piece placed in cell $ (i,j) $ can attack every cell $ (x,y) $ such that $ x \ge i $ and $ y \ge j $ , except for $ (i,j) $ itself.

For example, on a $ 10 \times 10 $ board, a piece placed at $ (3,4) $ can attack the cells highlighted below:

![](https://cdn.luogu.com.cn/upload/vjudge_pic/CF2219E/7d90b1fe95786bee605a4aba8a403495eb80ebff54a595a5d0e1223ccc35ac63.png)Given a chessboard with some pieces placed on it, we say that a cell is good if the number of pieces that attack that cell is even.

Construct a configuration of pieces such that every cell is good, regardless of whether the cell itself contains a piece or not, and the board contains at least $ \lfloor \frac{n^2}{10} \rfloor \cdot 3 $ pieces.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 50 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains an integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 5000 $ ).

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n^2 $ over all test cases is at most $ 25\,000\,000 = 5000^2 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, print $ n $ lines. In the $ i $ -th line, print $ n $ integers separated by spaces, where a $ 1 $ represents that there is a piece in that cell, and a $ 0 $ represents that there is not.

The $ j $ -th integer in the $ i $ -th line corresponds to cell $ (i,j) $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
1
3
Output
0 0 1
0 1 1
1 0 1

Note
This example is a chessboard of dimensions $ 3 \times 3 $ where every cell is good.

Cell $ (3, 3) $ is good because the cells with pieces $ (1, 3), (2, 3), (2, 2), (3, 1) $ attack it, and this count is 4, which is even.

Cell $ (1, 3) $ is good because no piece attacks it, so the count of pieces that attack this cell is 0.

Cell $ (3, 2) $ is good, and notice that it is required for that cell to be good even though it does not have a piece.

hints.title: Hint1
hints.content: Can you rewrite the condition on the board to something simpler?

hints.title: Hint2
hints.content: Label the board $$$v_{i,j}$$$ where $$$v_{0,0}$$$ is the top left cell. We work mod $$$2$$$. A board $$$v_{i,j}$$$ is valid if and only if $$$v_{i,j}+v_{i+1,j}+v_{i,j+1}=0$$$ and all entries above the secondary diagonal are zero.

hints.title: Hint3
hints.content: Warm up: try to get a construction of density $$$1/4$$$.

hints.title: Hint4
hints.content: The next few hints get quite spoilery. If you don't want to see them yet but are stuck, consider coding some kind of heuristic (say, simulated annealing or A*) and running it on small values of $$$n$$$ to see what the result looks like.

hints.title: Hint5
hints.content: Prove that the density is at most $$$1/3$$$.

hints.title: Hint6
hints.content: Consider the grid $$$w_{i,j}$$$ with $$$w_{i,j}=1$$$ when $$$i+j\ge n-1$$$ and $$$i\not\equiv j\pmod 3$$$, and $$$w_{i,j}=0$$$ otherwise.

hints.title: Hint7
hints.content: This grid is almost valid: $$$v_{i,j}+v_{i+1,j}+v_{i,j+1}=1$$$ only for very few concrete positions $$$(i,j)$$$. Try to fix the issues at those positions in a way that does not disturb much of the grid.

hints.title: Hint8
hints.content: For each position $$$(i_0, j_0)$$$ on the diagonal where $$$w_{i_0,j_0} = 0$$$, we want to add a pattern to the grid that fixes the issues there.

hints.title: Hint9
hints.content: For each such $$$(i_0,j_0)$$$, flip $$$w_{i_0,j_0}$$$ and add a Pascal triangle mod $$$2$$$ to the grid.

hints.title: Hint10
hints.content: To implement this in $$$O(n^2)$$$, instead of adding each pascal independently, try to compute the sum of all of the pascals directly.

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2219E - Weird Chessboard
We label the grid $$$v_{i, j}$$$ with $$$v_{0, 0}$$$ being the top left corner. From now on, we work modulo $$$2$$$ when operating with $$$v$$$.
Claim.
A filling of the grid is valid if and only if $$$v_{i, j}=0$$$ whenever $$$i+j \lt n-1$$$, and $$$v_{i, j}+v_{i+1, j}+v_{i, j+1}=0$$$ for all $$$0\le i,j \lt n-1$$$.
We are going to restrict ourselves to fillings where $$$v_{i, n-i-1} = 1$$$ for all $$$i$$$. It is easy to see that any optimal filling must be of this kind.
We will define a preliminary filling $$$w_{i,j}$$$ as
1 if $$$i+j\ge n-1$$$ and $$$i\not\equiv j \pmod 3$$$,
0 otherwise.
The idea is that $$$w$$$ has density $$$1/3$$$, and only fails to be valid at a few points, namely near the diagonal. We will fix $$$w$$$ in a way that leaves a chunk of the grid unchanged.
We introduce what we do with a motivating example in the $$$n=16$$$ case.
The first figure is the original $$$w$$$. We look at position $$$(9,6)$$$. There are three issues around this position: $$$$$$ w_{9, 6} = 0, $$$$$$ $$$$$$ w_{9, 6} + w_{9, 5} + w_{10, 5} = 1, $$$$$$ $$$$$$ w_{9, 6} + w_{8, 6} + w_{8, 7} = 1. $$$$$$ To fix these issues, we add the pattern in the second figure to $$$w$$$. This pattern comes from the well-known Pascal triangle, considered mod $$$2$$$, plus flipping position $$$w_{9, 6}$$$. Formally, the pattern in the second figure is given by $$$u_{9,6} = 1$$$ and $$$$$$ u_{9+i+1, 6+j} \equiv \binom{i}{j} \pmod{2}\ \forall i,j\ge 0. $$$$$$ The rightmost figure is $$$u+w$$$, where the three issues around $$$w_{9, 6}$$$ are now fixed. The key observation is that our transformation only affects the region of the board below the diagonal, and below and to the left of $$$(9,6)$$$.
In general, we will do as follows: iterate over the points $$$(i_0, j_0)$$$ of the diagonal with $$$w_{i_0, j_0} = 0$$$. For each we do as follows. If $$$i_0 \ge j_0$$$, as in the example, we flip $$$w_{i_0, j_0}$$$ and add to $$$w$$$ the pattern $$$$$$ u_{i_0+1+a, j_0+b} = \binom{a}{b}\pmod 2 $$$$$$ for each $$$a,b$$$ with $$$0\le b\le a$$$ for which $$$(i_0+1+a, j_0+b)$$$ lies in the grid. If $$$i_0  \lt  j_0$$$, we add a similar pattern above and to the right of $$$(i_0, j_0)$$$. Formally, if $$$i_0  \lt  j_0$$$, we flip $$$w_{i_0, j_0}$$$ and add to $$$w$$$ the pattern $$$$$$ u_{i_0+a, j_0+1+b} = \binom{b}{a}\pmod 2 $$$$$$ for each $$$0\le a\le b$$$ for which $$$(i_0+a, j_0+1+b)$$$ lies in the grid.
After the procedure is over, $$$w$$$ is a valid grid. Note that naively implementing the above is $$$O(n^3)$$$, which won't pass. We take care of implementation at the end of the solution.
Claim.
After the process, $$$w$$$ has density at least $$$0.3$$$.
Proof.
We actually do not have a formal proof of this fact. We instead give the intuition behind the claim and an heuristic "proof".
By our construction, the cells $$$(i,j)$$$ with $$$i,j\ge n/2$$$ have been left unchanged, so the density is still $$$2/3$$$ among those cells. For the rest of cells with $$$i+j\ge n-1$$$, one can a priori guess the density will be at least around $$$1/2$$$. Hence, the final density should be around $$$$$$ \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{\frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{3}}{2} = \frac{7}{24} \approx 0.292. $$$$$$ In practise, the final density happens to be slightly larger than $$$7/24$$$, of about $$$3/10$$$. The following figures showcase our construction for $$$N = 512$$$ and $$$N = 700$$$.
In the middle square, as said, we get $$$2/3$$$ density. The bottom left and top right triangles expand following a fractal. First, let us look at the $$$512$$$ (power of two) case. Here, the density should be exactly $$$0.3$$$. Why? If $$$d$$$ is the density of the bottom-left (or top right) triangles, then we should have something like $$$$$$ f = \frac{1}{4}\left(f + f + \frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{4}(f + f + 0 + f)\right), $$$$$$ where the first two summands are for to left and top subtriangles, the third summand is for the central triangle, and the final summand is for the bottom right subtriangle. Solving the equation yields $$$f = 8/15$$$. Thus, the density of the whole grid is $$$$$$ \frac{1}{2}\left(f + \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{10}{30}. $$$$$$ In the $$$n = 700$$$ (not power of two) case, the fractal gets cut off. However, intuitively, the density should be larger than $$$0.3$$$, since the extra part that you gain between $$$n = 512$$$ and $$$n = 700$$$ is very dense. This also holds in practise: our construction is denser than $$$0.3$$$ whenever $$$n$$$ is far from being a power of two.
Finally, let us look at implementation. We already said the naive $$$O(n^3)$$$ will not pass, we will do the job in $$$O(n^2)$$$. Define a vector $$$s$$$ which is $$$1$$$ at positions $$$(i_0+1,j_0)$$$ where $$$(i_0,j_0)$$$ is a zero on the diagonal with $$$i_0\ge j_0$$$, and at positions $$$(i_0,j_0+1)$$$ where $$$(i_0,j_0)$$$ is a zero on the diagonal with $$$i_0  \gt  j_0$$$, and let $$$s$$$ be $$$0$$$ everywhere else. Note that these points where $$$s$$$ is $$$1$$$ are the peaks of the pascal triangles we're adding to $$$w$$$ in our construction. Now, we propagate in $$$s$$$ the Pascal triangle recurrence downward and to the left from the position $$$(i_0+1,j_0)$$$ with lowest $$$i_0$$$, and upward to the right from the position $$$(i_0, j_0+1)$$$ with highest $$$i_0$$$. After this is done, $$$s$$$ is exactly the same as the sum of all the $$$u$$$ vectors we're adding in our construction. Thus adding $$$s$$$ to $$$w$$$ and flipping the zeros on the diagonal of $$$w$$$ results in our construction, and is $$$O(n^2)$$$.
